svg.title: Lynq AI architecture in plain language
svg.text: Lynq — AI architecture in plain English
Inputs come in (left) · models do specific jobs (middle) · clean structured outputs leave (right) · every output is logged + becomes training data (bottom).

g.text: Drop blurry shots · keep originals safe
Vision QC model · perceptual hash dedupe

svg.text: 3 · The 12 Lynq models (what each one does, in one sentence)

g.text: Lynq model zoo — each model is small, specialized, replaceable
A · Extraction · turn raw stuff into structured fields
Reads doctor's scrawl from a photo and types it out.
Returns: Dx, ICD, Rx, meds, confidence per field.
Tech: vision-language model fine-tuned on Indian medical script.
Starts as Claude/GPT vision, replaced by fine-tuned small model later.
Listens to doctor dictating · writes it out.
Same Dx/ICD/Rx output structure as handwriting.
Tech: Whisper-class STT with Hindi + English code-switch.
Reads PDFs and JPEGs · pulls fields out.
Tech: layout-aware OCR (LayoutLM family) + structured LLM extraction.
Looks at any image · says "this is an HPE / OT / consent / etc."
Tech: small CNN fine-tuned on labelled hospital doc corpus.
Runs at capture for instant tagging.
Reads the insurer's query · says which of 10 types it is.
Tech: prompt-based LLM → fine-tuned small model (Llama/Mistral).
Looks at a draft claim · predicts query probability.
"67% query risk — top reason: HPE missing".
Tech: XGBoost on 60+ tabular features (scheme, procedure, docs).
Estimates what the insurer will approve.
Expected ₹ · adj-factor anomaly flag · TAT in days.
Tech: gradient-boosted regression + isolation-forest anomaly check.
Watches OPD context · suggests likely Dx + required docs.
"Capture ECG, troponin now — needed for cashless".
Tech: RAG over scheme docs + chief-complaint LLM prompt chain.
C · Retrieval + drafting · find the evidence, write the response
Asked "do we have the HPE?" → answers with file + page.
Tech: pgvector embeddings + Elasticsearch BM25 + reranker.
The retrieval wedge — the simplest, highest-ROI module.
Clerk types "OT date?" · gets answer from folder.
Cites the source document + page number.
Tech: RAG over Evidence Index · LLM with grounding.
Refuses to answer if not in index (no hallucination).
Writes the reply to an insurer query · doctor-signable.
Template per query type + pre-attached evidence.
Tech: LLM with few-shot templates from historic responses.
Always editable by clerk + signed by doctor.
Tech: statistical anomaly (Prophet/STL) + Bayesian change point.
Only visible at network scale — competitor-proof.
D · Decision layer · combine model outputs into actions
Each model spits out a confidence score. The decision layer combines them with simple rules:
• If risk > 60% AND missing_doc model fires → block submit + send ward request.
• If classifier confidence < 70% on a query → human reviews before auto-draft.
• If Evidence Locator finds the doc with score > 0.85 → auto-attach. Otherwise show top-3 candidates to clerk.
Every output becomes a labelled training example over time.
The classifier guesses "missing_doc"; weeks later we see whether the insurer accepted the response. That outcome labels the original guess.
For rare queries: prompt LLM to generate 20-30 variants of each real case.
Validated by TPA clerk · <20% rejection rate target.
Lets us bootstrap from 1k real cases to ~14k training rows.
Anonymised data across hospitals → cross-scheme patterns no one else sees.
Each hospital's classifier benefits from others' outcomes.
v1 prompt-based 75% → v2 fine-tuned 88% → v3 custom 92% → v4 health-claims 96%.
Each tier unlocked by data volume — first mover gets to v4 first.
India's only labelled corpus of government health-claims queries.
Standard techniques · nothing exotic. Each model uses the simplest tool that works.
Claude / GPT with few-shot examples + structured
Used for: classifier v1, drafter, co-pilot, Ask Lynq.
LLM grounded in your folder + scheme docs.
Never hallucinates · always cites the source.
Used for: Evidence Locator, Ask Lynq, drafter, co-pilot.
Llama 3 / Mistral fine-tuned on your data.
Cheaper + faster than API calls at scale.
Replaces classifier + drafter as data grows.
Explainable: shows top features per prediction.
Used for: risk scorer, approval predictor.
Each doc page → a list of numbers.
pgvector inside Postgres · cheap + fast at our scale.
Elasticsearch BM25 over per-page OCR text.
Hybrid with vector search via reranker.
For images: Google Vision / Tesseract / Donut.
Layout-aware so tables and forms parse cleanly.
Spots spikes in denials, query patterns.
Cheap, explainable — no LLM needed here.
Lynq never sends a query response without doctor signature.
Never auto-submits a claim without TPA clerk approval.
All facts must cite a source document + page.
If Evidence Locator returns nothing → "not on file" (no guessing).
Drafter only fills templates with verified data.
PII stripped before anything goes to LLM provider.
Aadhaar hashed, names tokenised in prompts.
All AI calls logged to FHIR AuditEvent.
Compliance is a feature, not a tax.
Every model has a held-out test set.
A/B test new versions on 10% traffic first.
Drift monitors: alert if precision drops > 5%.
Rollback in < 1 hour if something breaks.
Layer 1 — raw stuff comes in (photos, voice, PDFs, query text).
Layer 2 — pre-processing cleans it up (drops blurry, detects language, strips PII, matches patient).
Layer 3 — twelve small models each do one specific job. Each is replaceable. Start with prompt-based LLMs; replace with fine-tuned smaller models as data grows.
Layer 4 — outputs are structured (JSON, citations, packets). Nothing free-form leaves the brain.
Layer 5 — every output is logged. Outcomes (insurer approve/reject) label the original prediction. Monthly retrain. Synthetic data fills gaps.
Layer 6 — standard techniques throughout (RAG, fine-tuning, XGBoost, embeddings, OCR, statistical anomaly). Nothing experimental.
Layer 7 — guardrails: human-in-loop, no hallucination, DPDP-safe, versioned + evaluated.
The whole architecture says one thing: small + specialised + grounded + logged. Lynq doesn't try to be one big model. It's a stack of cheap specialists that compound through the data flywheel.
